Public Benefit & Density Bonus Valuation Tool
Step 1 of 3 · Project

Define the Project & Bonus Request

Name the project, place it on the map to set the district and density pool, classify the use and construction cost, then enter the density request.

Location and Zoning

Drop the pin or search an address to pull site data.

Development Classification and Cost

Enter construction cost and market value directly, or use the estimators to derive them from specs.
Estimate construction cost from project specs
Converts $/unit to $/SF, seeded from the use type. Enter a $/SF to set the market value below.
Estimate market value from market sub-type

Density Request and Value

Auto-summed from your map parcel selection; type to override.
By-right plus bonus.

Density Pool and Scarcity

Bonus units draw from a finite pool. Units remaining come from the Allocation ledger; demand and horizon from Assumptions. The scarcer the pool, the higher the pool value added to the break-even floor — watch this update as you set the bonus request above.
Step 2 of 3 · Benefits

Build the Public Benefit Package

Add benefits and enter the quantity provided above what code already requires; each line is ranked by priority-weighted public value per developer dollar (the badge).

How lines are countedEach line has two per-unit figures: Developer cost / unit (what it costs the developer to provide it) and Public value / unit (what it is worth to the city) — the gap between them is the public's gain from the trade, and it varies by benefit (sometimes the city values a thing far above what it costs to build; sometimes not). Public value offered = quantity × public value per unit; lines are ranked by the top-right badge (priority-weighted public value per developer dollar). Enter only the amount beyond what code already requires — if code mandates part of a benefit, leave that part out so required work isn't credited as a voluntary concession. Full method in the User Guide; cost factors and the category priority weights (section D) are editable under Assumptions.
Developer cost
Public value offered
Benefit values come from the library. Add unlisted items by extending the library.
Step 3 of 3 · Results

Decision & Negotiation Range

Review the decision, then use the suggested counter-package and the ranked benefits to shape the ask.

Zone of Possible Agreement (ZOPA)

Developer benefit spending, from the minimum that satisfies the city up to the value to developer. The shaded band is the target capture zone — a fair-share guideline, not adopted code (analyst overlay), off the absolute floor and ceiling.

Suggested Counter-Package

The most plain-value-efficient library benefits, sized to clear the break-even floor within the developer's budget. A negotiation aid, not a recommendation; add it to whatever is already on the Benefits step and adjust.

Where the Bonus Value Goes

The sale value of the bonus units, split into build-and-sell cost, developer profit, public benefits secured, and value kept by the developer. A bigger teal slice means more captured for the public.

Benefits Ranked by Value-to-Cost Ratio

City value per dollar of developer cost. Use it to decide what to ask for; size the ask with the explorer below.

Deal Explorer

Drag to test how much benefit spending the deal needs, at a fixed value ratio. It doesn’t change your entered package.

Diagnostics

Fiscal Snapshot & Counterfactual
Taxable value / acre, plus a do-nothing-vs-by-right-vs-bonus property-tax comparison — broken out by level of government (city / county / schools / other local) so staff can see how little of the ad-valorem the city keeps — and the "cost of saying no." Context only, never part of the decision. Enter the current parcel assessed value below (county autofill or manual).
Cross-Check: Tampa 10:1 / ICC Construction-Cost Basis
An optional cross-check against the basis SPB Planning cited (Tampa Code 27-140 and ICC). It compares the developer’s benefit spend to the bonus units' construction cost. Reference only, not part of the decision. Inputs editable under Assumptions § F.
Robustness (±20% Sketch Sensitivity)
Moves each headline figure 20% the wrong way at once and reports whether the decision survives. Applied around the midpoint estimates, not stacked on the conservative low end the decision already uses, so the two cautions don't compound.

How Value to Developer Is Calculated

How the Break-Even Floor Is Calculated

How the Offered Public Value Is Calculated

Settings

Assumptions

Sketch-grade defaults, mostly uncalibrated to local data; edit any value and it flows through the model. Category multipliers set how much more the city values a dollar in each category than plain cash.

Settings

Benefit Library

The city’s value of each benefit (Low/High/Applied/Override; Applied is the midpoint unless overridden), seeding the city value when you add it. The developer’s cost is not set here, it’s their per-project proposal on the Benefits step. Placeholders, calibrate to local bids. For resilience benefits (seawall, living shoreline, stormwater, dune), the most defensible public value is the avoided-loss benefit from a FEMA-style benefit-cost analysis (e.g. the SFRPC / AECOM Resilience BCA tool), not a per-unit placeholder.

Reference

User Guide & Method

How this tool prices a density-bonus deal, what each number means, the limits to keep in mind, and the legal guardrails that sit on top of the local code.

Purpose

Bonus density is a negotiation: the city grants units beyond what zoning allows by right — a finite, public asset — in exchange for public benefits. The central questions are how much that density is worth and what the city should require in return. A developer typically brings a pro forma to that discussion; staff often do not, which makes it difficult to size either the ask or the offer consistently. Without a shared basis, the city can require less than the density is worth, or more than a feasible project can support. This tool provides a consistent, documented starting point: an estimate of the bonus's value to the developer (the walk-away ceiling), the minimum the city can lawfully require (the impact-mitigation floor, plus the scarcity cost of drawing down a finite pool), and whether a specific benefit package meets that threshold. It is a basis for discussion among staff, the applicant, and the Commission, not a substitute for their judgment.

It is a staff sketch tool, not an appraisal, pro forma, or legal determination — order-of-magnitude figures meant to frame the negotiation, not end it. It prices bonus allocations from the city's two finite pools (195-unit RU, 325-unit TLU) and Large Resort district-cap lodging (added by conditional use, so no pool-scarcity cost applies; the impact floor and developer ceiling still bound the deal). Where added density instead needs a comp-plan amendment or charter referendum, the tool flags that separate approval. Calibrate inputs to local data before relying on any number, and commission an independent feasibility review for any allocation large relative to the pool or marginal in its decision.

The Numbers, in Plain Terms

  • Value to developer. The most the bonus units are worth to the developer (discounted residual profit): the walk-away ceiling. Test the conservative low end; the high end is the negotiation target. Derived from a per-unit market value — entered as a sale comp or built up from an income approach on Step 1 (condo = $/SF; rental = NOI ÷ cap rate; lodging = ADR × occ × ancillary × EBITDA ÷ cap rate) — run through a residual cost build-up.
  • Pool value used. Value of the finite pool units the bonus consumes (pool pathway only). Near zero while the pool is plentiful; ramps as the balance falls below projected demand × horizon; at full depletion reaches the units' full value and the tool recommends Hold.
  • Impact offset (required by law). The developer's fair share of the traffic, utility, and service demand the new units create. Compellable whether or not a deal closes (F.S. §163.3180; Nollan / Dolan / Koontz).
  • Break-even floor. Impact offset plus pool value consumed. Below it, the public subsidizes the project.
  • Developer cost. What the offered benefit package costs the developer to provide.
  • Public value offered. The city's plain-dollar cost to provide the package (quantity above code × city $/unit). Benefits are credited at full value, secured as a condition of approval before the units release. This is what the decision tests against the floor.
  • Value ratio (the badge on each benefit). A ranking score: city value × the category's priority weight (Resilience 2.5×, Environmental 2.0×, … Cash 1.0×) ÷ developer cost. Higher means more priority-weighted public value per developer dollar. It orders benefits by efficiency and drives the suggested counter-package — but it does not enter the pass/fail verdict, which compares plain, unweighted public value to the floor. The priority weights are placeholders pending adopted city priorities, and are editable in Assumptions section D.

The Three Inputs That Matter Most

Market value (the ceiling), construction cost (the floor), and developer margin (the split) drive nearly every output — calibrate these first, under Assumptions. The scarcity exponent matters only near depletion.

How the Recommendation Is Reached

Worthwhile when public value offered meets the break-even floor. Feasible when developer cost stays under the value to developer, tested on its conservative low end. The agreement zone runs from the lowest worthwhile spend up to that ceiling — fill it with the highest value-ratio benefits first.

Known Limits

  • Plain-dollar decision. Pass/fail compares the package's plain public value to the plain-dollar floor. Category multipliers (resilience 2.5×, etc.) only rank benefits within a package; they do not inflate the offer.
  • Invented curve shapes. The scarcity exponent and value-decline factor are judgment shapes, not empirical (both editable under Assumptions).
  • Margin is not a return. A flat percent of cost proxying a 12–15% IRR target; it ignores financing, hold period, and time value. Treat the ceiling as order-of-magnitude.
  • Land held constant. Prices only the marginal bonus units; does not subtract land (a by-right sunk cost), so it understates all-in cost and is not a residual land value.
  • Double-counting trap. Enter only the quantity above what code already requires, or code-mandated work gets credited as a concession.
  • Optional two-tier cost. A construction step-up can be charged to just the marginal bonus units (default 1.00×; set roughly 1.10–1.15× to reflect added density pushing the project into a costlier construction type or structured parking).
  • Valuations assume delivery. Condition allocations on security (letter of credit or bond), delivery timing, and recalculation if approved density rises.
  • Single TLU pool. All lodging scarcity is priced against the 325-unit pool; the separate TC-1 (50) and Boutique Hotel-Condo (125) pools are not modeled (Step 1 flags when they apply).
  • Residential and lodging only. Commercial components of mixed-use projects do not draw pool units and are out of scope.
  • Tax excluded. The Fiscal Snapshot shows taxable value and yield per acre as context only; counting it toward the ask would be fiscalization of land use.
  • Placeholders. Hard costs, category multipliers, impact figures, and district densities are placeholders pending local cost data, a current concurrency study, and adopted priorities. Confirm the district against the official FLU map.

Basis

Added units come only from finite pools created by the 2007 down-zoning of Large Resort (65.16 ac): the 195-unit RU pool (LDC §39.18) and the 325-unit transient-lodging pool for TC-2 / Activity Center / Bayou Residential (FLU element). Each allocation is by Commission ordinance plus conditional use on a "merit basis" (LDC §4.12(d)), balance recited per §39.18(c); qualifying benefits are listed in §4.12(d)(1)–(9). Impact figures use SPB's adopted fees: the Pinellas Multimodal fee (Code §150-40) ≈ $1,248/condo, $1,420/MF, $1,604/hotel room, plus the parks/rec fee ≈ $843/residential unit (lodging exempt; Ord. 2022-04). Pinellas levies no school, parks, fire, or library impact fee, so the § E High end carries a flagged proportionate-share estimate a concurrency study should replace.

What code does not set. No contribution rate, benefit-valuation formula, floor, or ceiling. The only statutory weighting is Chapter 163 proportionate-share, the basis for the impact floor. The ZOPA bounds and the 15–25% / 25–50% guardrails are analytic overlays, not adopted code.

Legal & Political Guardrails

Surfaced as caveats, not enforced in the math. Route the exaction framing through the City Attorney before treating any number as actionable.

  • Charter referendum. The SPB charter requires a voter referendum before the Commission can increase height, density, or intensity, or change a land-use category. A recommended bonus may not be grantable by staff or Commission action alone.
  • Live Local Act (2023, amended 2024) can override local density and height for qualifying affordable projects (broadly, 40%+ of units at or below 120% AMI for 30+ years) — the developer's by-right outside option, which caps how much public benefit the city can extract.
  • Exactions doctrine (Nollan / Dolan / Koontz / Sheetz). Any required benefit must satisfy essential nexus and rough proportionality, extended to monetary exactions by Koontz (2013) and to legislative fee schedules by Sheetz (2024). Exposure is highest when a benefit is priced off land-value uplift rather than impacts, which is why the floor is sized on impact mitigation and uplift is used only as a ceiling.
  • State offset rule §166.04151. If the city ever mandates affordable units, it must fully offset the developer's cost. Voluntary bonus density is a different posture, but a mandate changes the math.

Not legal advice. Confirm density / FAR figures and pool balances against the adopted Comprehensive Plan and the live Municode LDC.

Ledger

Allocation History

Every conditional-use permit that drew from the pool. Approved allocations reduce the remaining pool and drive the scarcity cost on Results. Add a row per decision.

Compare

Saved Scenarios

Snapshot inputs and results and compare side by side. Figures are frozen at save time under the version shown per row. Loading replaces the current inputs and re-evaluates under today's methodology, so an older snapshot may read differently, by design.
